# Teaching Claude To Remember: Part 3 — Sessions And Resumable Workflow

[![Nick Porter](https://miro.medium.com/v2/da:true/resize:fill:32:32/0*rZ7mPK45Co0PX60-)](https://medium.com/@porter.nicholas?source=post_page---byline--1c356d9e442f---------------------------------------)

[Nick Porter](https://medium.com/@porter.nicholas?source=post_page---byline--1c356d9e442f---------------------------------------)

Follow

7 min read

·

Jan 4, 2026

3

2

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D1c356d9e442f&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40porter.nicholas%2Fteaching-claude-to-remember-part-3-sessions-and-resumable-workflow-1c356d9e442f&source=---header_actions--1c356d9e442f---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

Hiya! My name is Nick and if you have been following my series on Claude Code you know now is when we get to the fun stuff. Adding the base structure for CLAUDE.md (part 1) and then strapping on the Memory system (part 2) was critical to getting this system ready for what makes your workflow really start clicking … Sessions.

So what does Anthropic say about Sessions?

### What Anthropic Officially Supports

`claude --continue` — Continue most recent conversation

`claude --resume` — Show conversation picker

`claude --resume <session-id>` — Resume specific session

`/rename` — Name a session for easy finding

So pretty cool … but how does this work?

All conversations are auto-saved with FULL message history. When you resume, the ENTIRE context is restored. Messages, tool usage, results. This boggles my mind a little coming from a traditional CS background and understanding that all those zeros and ones are sitting out on some server some place … terrifying and also super useful.

> Key quote from Anthropic:
>
> “Name sessions early: Use /rename when starting work on a distinct task — it’s much easier to find ‘payment-integration’ than ‘explain this function’ later.”

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*k1gqjtPitXytTEj73Ar9eA.png)

Image credit Gemini AI

### What Anthropic Gives You (And Where It Stops)

So Claude Code has built in Session management. You run `claude --resume` to pickup on your last conversation and your context is restored — messages, tool results, everything.

> Context gets convoluted, twisted and often disoriented

This is great for short term workflow continuity. Stepped away for lunch? Run `claude --continue`. Your IDE crashed again like mine does after I load up a bunch of important stuff? Run `claude --resume`.

There are a few problems with this though:

1) **Context gets convoluted**, twisted and often disoriented leading to more potential hallucinations. What this ultimately means in a production code environment is _‘your bot just went bonkers and did something stupid’_.

2) Long-running sessions are **not shareable amongst teammates**.

3) **Conversations are messy**. If your scrolling through the conversation history after maybe 3–4 days tops almost ALL of that resembles unusable and un-resumable effort.

4) **No structured hand-off**. What files changed, what gotchas were hit (lessons learned), so for any given project what work remains?

[**Common workflows - Claude Code Docs** \\
\\
**Learn about common workflows with Claude Code.**\\
\\
code.claude.com](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows?source=post_page-----1c356d9e442f---------------------------------------)

### Today’s Limitations

I say today’s limits because in a week or two Claude Code might have their own built in Session system. If so ours will snap right into place and be usable from Day 1!

Anthropic’s `--resume` continues the same conversation with the same context. But what happens when:

— Your context has ALREADY filled up and compacted once or twice :(

— You’re ready to hand the work you had in-flight to someone else

— It’s been a day or two and you can’t recall EXACTLY the conversation you had or **you’re in a rush** and want to just blurt out what you want!

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*2aCpYDyrizlo_KFqDEqcRA.png)

Image credit Gemini AI

### When A Session File System Became Necessary

I have used a couple AI coding tools extensively. Namely Roo Code and Claude Code. I suffered innumerable conversations where I felt like I was re-explaining something I had repeated AT LEAST 3–4 times already. This happened in work that was in-flight or completed work and most often with bugs. I revisited the same exact bugs so many times, fixed it with TDD a FEW times haha, still came back to the same bug.

## Get Nick Porter’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

I knew I needed to dig into Anthropic’s docs and find a Session File system that worked. A system that could build off of the official Anthropic framework and features as well as expected enhancements. Let’s face it Claude Code on its own is great, but can be a LOT better with the right guidance. Let’s take a look at the gaps.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*0mdzsx3ZrjUAUJBB_xAUXA.png)

Image credit Gemini

### Session File System And Structure

It’s important to have a rigid structure for your session file system to make it more predictable for Claude when reviewing old sessions. In order to consistently produce and include in the push session files so that memory is truly preserved over time you need to specify a template that all Claude instances should use.

### Session File Structure

.claude/memory/sessions/

\| — TEMPLATE-MINIMAL.md # 40 lines — short tasks

\| — TEMPLATE.md #136 lines — complex features

\| — 2025–12–04-author-rotation.md

\| — 2025–12–08-calendar-sync.md

\*\*Naming convention\*\*: `YYYY-MM-DD-topic-name.md`

> Our Session system is sortable by date, searchable by topic and self-documenting

### The Minimal Template

Truthfully many tasks are NOT long-running tasks. A lot of the time it’s a quick bug fix, wording adjustment or maybe small UX fixes you notice similar to that bathroom tile project you took on and now notice faults in … wait that’s me. So there ARE times when I don’t even create a session file.

Truthfully though since Session files are only consumed on demand the benefit outweighs the disadvantages in the form of disk space taken up on your mac (should be cause they run AI tools better :)).

```
  # Session: [YYYY-MM-DD] - [Topic Name]

  ## What Was Done
  [2-3 sentences: What was accomplished this session]

  ## Files Changed
  - `src/path/to/file.ts` - [brief description of change]
  - `src/path/to/file.ts` - [brief description of change]

  ## Gotchas Hit
  ### [Title] (optional - only if encountered)
  **Problem**: [what went wrong]
  **Fix**: [how to avoid next time]

  ## Next Steps
  1. [Immediate next action - be specific!]
  2. [Second priority]
  3. [Third if needed]

  ## Critical Context
  - [Constraint future Claude must know]
  - [Decision that affects implementation]

  ## Quality Status
  | Gate  | Status |
  |-------|--------|
  | Tests | Pass/Fail |
  | Types | Pass/Fail |
  | Lint  | Pass/Fail |

  ---
  **Commit**: [hash or "Pending"]
```

### Long-Running Tasks And TEMPLATE.md

For really long-running tasks such as new feature additions running the Session file system is absolutely critical ya’ll. Claude sessions can end abruptly or go sideways after multiple compactions. There must be a clear hand-off strategy in order to allow a multi-session and multi-agent workflow succeed.

> If your Next Steps require Claude to ask “what does that mean?” — they’re not specific enough.

Providing Claude with detail for what should be done next is critical. Previous Claude shouldn’t leave a trail of breadcrumbs that could get swept away or that lead the next Claude off-course.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*vFB5OboqzukpX7mkXfZDwA.png)

### Time To Kick The Tires

So let’s test it! Now that you have the files in place you will need to gen up a new Claude session and fire off a new prompt to test the system and see if it works!

**How to test this thing:**

1) Do some work with Claude around the 15+ minute mark. This shouldn’t be research work but instead coding preferably where your fixing something or improving a feature

2) Ask Claude to create a session file

3) run `/clear` to wipe context

4) Ask: “Read the session file and tell me what’s next”

### The Hand-off That Works

Before the Session File system closing Claude meant losing all the context I had built up to that point, some good some maybe clutter but still there was valuable stuff there! If you can count on all your fingers and toes the times you repeated yourself to Claude then I congratulate you. I have gotten so close to rage-quitting because of the number of threads I have repeated … over and over before using the Session File system.

### Circling Back

So now we have completed the third part of our series on teaching Claude to remember.

Part 1 — The base architecture

Part 2 — Adding Memory

Part 3 — Adding a Session File system

### Up Next!

Next we will be discussing Adding Domain. This is when we will discuss Skills. When you should use them, how to create them and why they are different than the other tools in the Anthropic Claude Code tool set.

Skills are quickly becoming one of my favorite tools in our toolbelt because they offer such a deep set of abilities. Those that can sort of snap in automatically to your workflow and those that offer very specific and narrow goals.

I’d love to hear your experience! Please offer feedback in the comments and tell me how you are implementing Session. Have you put THIS system in? If so how can it be improved? Let’s make this thing kick ass!
